coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous monitoring of mitochondrial viscosity and membrane potential based on fluorescence changing and location switching of carbon dots in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Shuo
Sun, Yuanqiang
Wu, Zhaofan
Yang, Ran
Qu, Lingbo
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: Mitochondrial viscosity and mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) are vital parameters for reflecting mitochondrial conditions and they are closely correlative in various biological events. Accordingly, simultaneous detection of mitochondrial viscosity and MMP is imperative for elucidating their interactions, further exploring the mechanism of diseases, and investigating numerous pathological procedures. However, such profound research remains a big challenge due to the lack of high-performing single ﬂuorescent probes capable of simultaneously visualizing of these two parameters. In this study, we developed a new kind of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) that can successfully realize simultaneous monitoring of mitochondrial viscosity and MMP in living cells. The fluorescence of CDs can be specifically turned on as the viscosity of solvents increases from 0.89 to 945 cP. Meanwhile, the CDs accumulate in mitochondria under normal MMP conditions, switch to exist in both mitochondria and nucleoli under reduced MMP, and present only in nucleoli with the vanishment of MMP. Thus, various MMP situations can be easily differentiated through the changed spatial distributions of CDs. More importantly, based on the developed CDs, an increased mitochondrial viscosity accompanying a decreased MMP was simultaneously tracked in drug-stimulated cells. We envision that the developed CDs has a great potential to be utilized as a promising fluorescent probe for mitochondrion-related biology studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mitochondrial membrane potential
Mitochondrial viscosity
Simultaneous detection
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurements of fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetime
Spatial locations of CDs with different MMP
Simultaneous detection of viscosity and MMP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [ 21974125 ], Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Henan Province ( 22TRTSTHN002 ), the 111 Project of Henan Province [ CXJD2021001 ], and the Collaborative Innovation Project of Zhengzhou ( Zhengzhou University ) [ 18XTZX12002 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs (inset: HRTEM image of CDs). (B) Zeta potential of CDs. (C) FTIR spectrum of CDs. (D) XPS spectrum of CDs.

(A) The absorption spectra, fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of CDs. (B) Excitation-independent fluorescence spectra of CDs. (C) The fluorescence spectra and (D) linear relationship between log I
546 and log η of CDs in water-glycerol mixtures with different proportions (0%–99%). (E) The fluorescence decay spectra and (F) linearity of log τ and log η of CDs in various viscosities (0%–80% glycerol).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and (B) fluorescent emission spectra of CDs in different solvents (1. CH2Cl2, 2. Methanol, 3. 1,4-dioxane, 4. N, N-dimethyl formamide, 5. DMSO, 6. THF, 7. Glycol, 8. H2O, 9. acetonitrile, 10. ethanol, 11. glycerol). (C) The pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs at different viscosities. (D) The photo-stability of CDs at different viscosities. (E) The fluorescence signals of CDs in the presence of amino acids, mental ions, ROS, and RSS at various viscosity of solvents (1. CDs, 2. Lys, 3. Arg, 4. Thr, 5. Ser, 6. His, 7. Pro, 8. Gln, 9. Met, 10. Val, 11. Tyr, 12. Leu, 13. Asp, 14. K+, 15. Ca2+, 16. Na+, 17. Mg2+, 18. Fe2+, 19. Fe3+, 20. Cu2+, 21. Zn2+, 22. Ba2+ 23. GSH, 24. Cys, 25. ascorbic acid, 26. H2O2, 27. Hcy).
A concentration string of confocal images of HeLa cells incubated with CDs. Scale bar: 25 μm.

(A) Confocal fluorescent images of HeLa cells cultured with CDs and MTDR, (B) Hoechst, or (C) LTDR. (a1-c1) Fluorescent images of CDs in cells. (a2-c2) The staining of MTDR, Hoechst, LTDR in HeLa cells. (a3-c3) Overlay images of CDs and MTDR, Hoechst, or LTDR. (a4-c4) Fluorescence correlation of CDs and MTDR, Hoechst, or LTDR. (a5-c5) Fluorescence intensity tendency of regions of interest of CDs and MTDR, Hoechst, or LTDR (green line in a3-c3). Scale bar: 20 μm.

(A) HeLa cells were treated with CDs for 30 min, and then cultured with CCCP (10 μM) for 5 min. (B) Confocal images of HeLa cells stained with CDs exclusively. (C) HeLa cells were pretreated with 4% paraformaldehyde for 30 min and subsequently stained with CDs. Enlarged area of the mitochondria (the first row) and the nucleus (the third row). Scale bar: 10 μm.

(A) Schematic illustration of the simultaneously monitoring of mitochondrial viscosity and MMP using CDs. (B–D) Fluorescent confocal images of HeLa cells treated with CDs only, monensin and CDs, and LPS and CDs. Enlarged area of the mitochondria (the first row) and the nucleus (the third row). Scale bar: 15 μm.

(A) Synthetic route of CDs and turn-on fluorescence of CDs with increasing viscosity. (B) Illustration of simultaneous monitoring of mitochondrial viscosity dynamics and MMP through fluorescence variation and location switching using the CDs.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous monitoring of mitochondrial viscosity and membrane potential based on fluorescence changing and location switching of carbon dots in living cells

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Shuo Guo and Yuanqiang Sun contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mitochondrial viscosity and mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) are vital parameters for reflecting mitochondrial conditions and they are closely correlative in various biological events. Accordingly, simultaneous detection of mitochondrial viscosity and MMP is imperative for elucidating their interactions, further exploring the mechanism of diseases, and investigating numerous pathological procedures. However, such profound research remains a big challenge due to the lack of high-performing single ﬂuorescent probes capable of simultaneously visualizing of these two parameters. In this study, we developed a new kind of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) that can successfully realize simultaneous monitoring of mitochondrial viscosity and MMP in living cells. The fluorescence of CDs can be specifically turned on as the viscosity of solvents increases from 0.89 to 945 cP. Meanwhile, the CDs accumulate in mitochondria under normal MMP conditions, switch to exist in both mitochondria and nucleoli under reduced MMP, and present only in nucleoli with the vanishment of MMP. Thus, various MMP situations can be easily differentiated through the changed spatial distributions of CDs. More importantly, based on the developed CDs, an increased mitochondrial viscosity accompanying a decreased MMP was simultaneously tracked in drug-stimulated cells. We envision that the developed CDs has a great potential to be utilized as a promising fluorescent probe for mitochondrion-related biology studies.

The carbon dots were elaborately designed for simultaneous determination of mitochondrial viscosity and membrane potential through their fluorescence variation and location migration from mitochondria to nucleoli.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The appropriate viscosity of the mitochondrial matrix and membrane potential are fundamental for maintaining mitochondrial function and they are tightly interrelated in diverse biological procedures [1–5]. For instance, apoptosis is firstly performed from the mitochondria along with a decreased MMP. During cell apoptosis, mitochondrial membrane permeability changes suddenly and the swelling of mitochondria will lead to the variation of viscosity [6–8]. Mitochondrial viscosity usually enhances in inflammation. Intriguingly, the inflammation usually occurs in the procedure of cell necrosis, which is often accompanied by a reduced MMP [9,10]. In addition, abnormal mitochondrial viscosity obstructs electron transport, disrupts the respiratory state, and may further induces disease of cancer [11–13]. Meanwhile, the MMP in cancer cells is often higher than normal cells for at least 60 mV due to the enhanced metabolism [14,15]. Therefore, the simultaneous detection of the real-time dynamic patterns in both mitochondrial viscosity and MMP is of immense importance to explore their relationships.

Hitherto, fluorescence imaging technology has become versatile tools for bioimaging due to the superiorities of temporal resolution, superb sensitivity, technical simplicity, noninvasiveness, and so on [16–21]. Some small-molecule fluorescent probes have been constructed to individually detect mitochondrial viscosity dynamics or MMP. For instance, Tang et al. synthesized a near-infrared probe to explore the effect of heavy mental ions and hydrogen peroxide on mitochondrial viscosity [1]. Lin's group designed a dual-emissive probe to visualize MMP [22]. Nevertheless, the simultaneous visualization of mitochondrial viscosity and MMP has not been realized. Technically, it is quite challenging to precisely acquire the two parameters of mitochondrial viscosity and MMP at the same time by combinating multiple fluorescent probes, because different probes have uneven localization, tedious staining process, superposed cytotoxicity, cross-talk, and different metabolism, which tend to produce false signals and affect the results seriously [23]. Hence, it is precious to prepare single fluorescent probe to synchronously visualize mitochondrial viscosity and MMP. To the best of our knowledge, such a fluorescent probe has not been reported yet.

Carbon dots (CDs), integration superiorities of splendid fluorescence properties, easy preparation and low cost, and high biocompatibility, have been applied in broad fields, including optoelectronics, biological imaging and sensing, and enzyme mimics [24–27]. All these developments inspire us that CDs could act as a powerful tool to track biological activities and reflect their associations. Herein, CDs capable of simultaneous monitoring mitochondrial viscosity and MMP were elaborately designed and synthesized. We adopted the citric acid and 1-phenylpyrrolidine as precursors since Friedel-Crafts reaction occurred between them under high temperature and pressure conditions. In addition, the citric acid contains carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which are beneficial for dehydration and carbonization to form CDs (Scheme 1 ). Pyrrolidine groups were used as molecular rotators to construct fluorescent viscosity-sensitive CDs. Apart from that, the intrinsic positive charges on the surface of CDs promoted them distribute in the mitochondria due to electrostatic interaction in normal MMP. Intriguingly, strong fluorescence emission of CDs was restored at high viscosity because of the restriction of non-radiative relaxation of pyrrolidine groups (Fig. S1), and the migration of CDs from the mitochondria to nucleoli emerged with reduced MMP. Thus, fluctuation of mitochondrial viscosity and MMP was simultaneously visualized through the fluorescence changing as well as location transferring of CDs in subcellular organelles. Most importantly, an increased mitochondrial viscosity alongside with a decreased MMP was synchronously detected within drug-mediated cells, demonstrating the great potential of the CDs for elucidating the mechanism of mitochondrial viscosity and MMP involved diseases and biological processes.

The SOP microwave reactor (CEM, USA) was employed to prepare CDs. The cytotoxicity of CDs was monitored by the Spark multi-detection microplate reader (Austria). The size and morphologies of CDs were assessed through the transmission electron microscope (FEI-Tecnai G2, USA). The ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) was employed to acquire CDs' X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS). The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of CDs were recorded by the Bruker Tensor 27 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The fluorescence spectra, UV–vis absorption spectrum, nanosecond fluorescence lifetime spectra, and the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of CDs in different organic solvents were obtained through the F-4600 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan), the Agilent 8453 spectrophotometer (California, USA), and the FLS 980 single-photon counting system (Edinburgh Instruments, Livingston, UK), respectively. All the confocal images were taken by the TCS SP8 X confocal microscope (Leica, Wetzlar, Germany).

All the solvents and chemicals used in the study were commercially available without any other treatment. 1-Phenylpyrrolidine was obtained from Alfa Aesar Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The solvents, including acetonitrile, ethylene glycol, tetrahydrofuran, glycerol, dioxane, ethyl acetate, methanol, and acetone, were acquired from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All kinds of amino acids tested in the research were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Monensin was bought from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). LPS was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA).

The mixture of citric acid (15 mM) and 1-phenylpyrrolidine (30 mM) in ethanol was heated for 0.5 h at 140 °C within a microwave reactor. The obtained product was next purified through column chromatography, and ethanol was used as an eluent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetime
ce:section.ce:para: Solutions of CDs were obtained in water-glycerol systems with various viscosities. The compounds were mixed thoroughly using a tube roller, followed by tests of absorption spectra, fluorescence lifetime spectra, and fluorescence intensity spectra. The fluorescence emission wavelengths were collected with the range of 490–700 nm, with the fixed excitation wavelength at 470 nm.

The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CDs and the solvent viscosity or between fluorescence lifetime of CDs and the viscosity of solvents was correlated by the Förster-Hoffmann equation.
log
I
=
C
+
x
log
η
Where I and C indicate the fluorescence emission intensity and a constant, respectively, x and η stand for the sensitivity of CDs to viscosity and the value of viscosity, respectively.

In addition, Förster–Hoffmann plot was acquired by plotting log τ versus log η.
log
τ
=
C
+
x
log
η
In which η indicates the viscosity, τ represents the fluorescence lifetime, x is the sensitivity of the CDs to viscosity, and C is a constant.

The standard MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphe-nyltetrazolium bromide) assay was implemented to assess the cytotoxicity of CDs. Briefly, HeLa cells were incubated in the 96-well culture plate. Then, CDs of increasing concentrations (0–12 μg/mL) were kept in the plate for 24 h duration. The solution of MTT was added into the culture medium of wells. After waiting for 4 h, the culture medium was removed and DMSO was loaded into wells. The plate was shaking carefully to dissolve the crystals after culturing with DMSO for 10 min. Finally, the absorbance at 570 nm was detected to assess the viability of HeLa cells.

The cells, including HeLa, HepG-2, SMMC-7721, and HL-7702, investigated in the work were seeded into a culture dish and maintained in a culture medium for 24 h with 5% CO2 at 37 °C. The culture medium comprises Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% penicillin, and 1% streptomycin.

The cells (containing HeLa cells, HepG-2 cells, HL-7702 cells, and SMMC-7721 cells) were incubated with CDs (5 μg/mL) for 30 min. Then, the commercial dye of MitoTracker™ Deep Red (MTDR, 50 nM) was added into the cells for 20 min. After washing with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), the colocalization tests were carried out in the end. Similarly, after HeLa cells were stained with CDs, Hoechst 33342 (1 μg/mL) or LysoTracker™ Deep Red (LTDR, 50 nM) was cultured with the cells, and co-staining experiments were explored finally. λ ex at 476 nm for CDs, λ ex at 638 nm for MTDR and LTDR, λ ex at 405 nm for Hoechst 33342. λ em at 520–600 nm for CDs, λ em at 655–725 nm for MTDR and LTDR, λ em at 415–510 nm for Hoechst 33342.

Initially, HeLa cells were grown in the dish for 24 h. Then, the HeLa cells were incubated with monensin (10 μM) for 30 min or LPS (2 μg/mL) for 1 h and further cultured with CDs for another 30 min. Finally, the confocal image experiments were performed.

The embryos of zebrafish were purchased from Shanghai Feixi Biotechnology Co and maintained at 28.5 °C, the favorable reproductive conditions. The zebrafish were cultured in the solution of CDs for 30 min and further washed with tank water. In the end, confocal fluorescent images were captured with the Leica confocal microscope after the zebrafish were anesthetized using tricaine. In addition, the fluorescence changes associated with different drugs stimulation were particularly well assessed at the level of zebrafish. The zebrafish were soaked in a solution of monensin (10 μM) or LPS (4 μg/mL) for 30 min and loaded with CDs for an additional 30 min. Eventually, the fluorescent images of zebrafish were acquired. The process and content of the animal experiments were executed according to the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of Zhengzhou University, and were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Zhengzhou University.

The CDs were produced via an one-step microwave-assisted strategy. As indicated in Fig. S2, the fluorescence of CDs increased gradually with the extension of irradiation time, while kept almost unchanged when reaction time rised from 30 min to 60 min. In addition, the fluorescence of CDs firstly increased with the temperature varied from 100 °C to 140 °C and then decreased with the temperature rised from 140 °C to 180 °C. Thus, with the reaction conditions of 30 min and 140 °C, CDs possess optimal fluorescence, which is benefit to the application of cell imaging. The TEM images and size distribution histogram of CDs showed that the morphology was spherical (Fig. 1 A), and the average diameter was approximately 3.8 nm (Fig. S3). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of CDs (insets of Fig. 1A) exhibited a crystal spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the basal spacing of graphitic carbon [28]. Further, the zeta potential of CDs (Fig. 1B) was about +38.17 mV, indicating the positive charges on the surface of CDs [29].

To better illustrate the surface functional groups of CDs, FTIR and XPS spectroscopy were recorded. As shown in Fig. 1C, CDs exhibited characteristic absorption peaks at 1390 cm−1, 2924 cm−1, and 1609 cm−1, corresponding to C–N, –CH2-, and –CN stretching vibrations, respectively. The vibrational absorption peaks at 1729 cm−1 and 3420 cm−1 revealed the presence of carboxyl (-COOH) and hydroxyl (-OH) groups on the CDs [30]. Besides, The XPS spectrum (Fig. 1D) showed three peaks at 284.6 eV, 530.1 eV, and 398.8 eV ascribing to C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s, and the corresponding atomic contents were 69.33%, 8.91%, and 21.76%, respectively [31]. The weak peak at 973.2 eV could be attributed to O KLL Auger decay, which is commonly observed in XPS spectra of reported works [32–34]. The high-resolution C 1s curve of CDs (Fig. S4A) could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 288.7 eV, 286.2, and 284.6, representing the chemical structure of HO–CO, CN, and C–C/C–N, respectively. The spectrum of O 1s (Fig. S4B) was fitted into two peaks, confirming the presence of OC–O (533.0 eV) and C–O (531.7 eV) [35]. Two fitting peaks were acquired from the high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. S4C), which occurred at 399.2 eV and 400.8 eV, belonged to the pyridinic N and pyrrolic N [36]. The XPS results further verified the functional groups and element component of CDs, which supported the results of FTIR spectra as well.

With prepared CDs in hand, the optical characteristics were explored subsequently. The UV–vis spectra of CDs (Fig. 2 A) displayed two distinct peaks at 320 nm and 463 nm, which could be ascribed to π-π∗ (aromatic sp2 domains) and n–π∗ (carboxyl and C–O/CN) transition, respectively [37]. Moreover, the solubility of CDs in water was explored. As illustrated in Fig. S5, the linearly increasing absorbance associated with the enhanced concentration of CDs suggested the good water dispersibility of CDs. The fluorescent emission of CDs at 545 nm was observed under the optimal excitation of 470 nm, and the excitation-independent emission of CDs was acquired with the excitation scope varied from 460 nm to 500 nm (Fig. 2B), manifesting the fluorescence signal derived predominantly from surface defect [38]. The significant Stokes Shift (82 nm) could greatly reduce the self-absorption effect and improve the fidelity of images [39]. Next, the spectroscopic properties of CDs to viscosity were performed. As shown in Fig. S6, the absorption intensity of CDs showed a slight deviation in water-glycerol mixtures with varied volume ratios. In contrast, a progressively considerable rise was recorded in the emission profile (Fig. 2C), and the 90-fold fluorescence intensity enhancement was obtained in 99% glycerol compared to water. In addition, an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.99) of the fluorescence emission intensity (log I
545nm ) against viscosity (log η) from 0.89 to 945 cP (the range of intracellular viscosity in physiology and even pathology) was acquired by fitting the Förster-Hoffmann equation (Fig. 2D), confirming the excellent viscosity sensitivity of CDs [40]. Besides, the fluorescence lifetime (τ) of CDs in solutions of different viscosities was determined. As we all know, photon emission or non-radiative decay occur in the process of relaxation of excited state. With the increased viscosity, the non-radiative decay decreases while the photon emission enhances. Thus, we predicted that the fluorescence lifetime, which was defined as the average time of emitting photons that return from the excited state to the ground state, was also improved with the rised viscosity [41–43]. As displayed in Fig. 2E, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs grown from 3.39 ns to 8.48 ns with the increasing viscosity (glycerol: 0%–80%) and the good linear relationship (R2 = 0.93) between log τ and log η was described through Förster-Hoffmann equation (Fig. 2F), which presented the similar variation tendency with solvent viscosity toward fluorescence.

What's more, the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 3 A), fluorescence signal, and quantum yield (QY) of CDs (Table S1) in different solvents with different polarities were tested. As shown in Fig. 3B, CDs showed negligible fluorescence and low QY in other solvents compared with glycerol, indicating that the fluorescence of CDs was non-sensitive to solvent polarity and the CDs could accurately and selectively detect viscosity. To assess CDs stability, the effect of solution pH on fluorescence intensity was investigated subsequently. As demonstrated in Fig. 3C and Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity and absorbance of CDs in different glycerol-water systems remained relative stable in the physiological range of pH 4.0–9.0. Additionally, the fluorescence signals of CDs in different glycerol-water mixtures remained almost unchanged upon continuous irradiation for 1 h, suggesting the satisfactory photostability of CDs (Fig. 3D). Given that, in monitoring viscosity, the fluorescence signal of CDs was independent of polarity and pH, we proceeded to explore the influence of biologically related species on CDs. Experimental results (Fig. 3E, Figs. S8–10) confirmed that the fluorescence of CDs in various glycerol-water mixtures was inert to constituents containing metal ions, amino acids, reactive oxygen species (ROS), and reactive sulfur species (RSS). Encouraged by the desirable attributes of CDs, such as excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, and outstanding photostability, we next explored the performance of CDs in monitoring viscosity alterations in complex biological systems.

To evaluate the utility of CDs in bioimaging applications, the cytotoxicity of CDs was confirmed firstly by standard 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. Fig. S11 proved that 80% of HeLa cells survived even if they were cultured with CDs with a concentration of 12 μg/mL for 24 h. The concentration of CDs used in the study was set to be 5 μg/mL. Thus it could be concluded that the cytotoxicity of CDs was negligible, and they possessed great potential for cellular imaging. Fluorescence pictures of HeLa cells were recorded for a series of solutions owning different amounts of CDs (0–10 μg/mL). Fig. 4 revealed that fluorescence intensity in cells gradually increased with rising concentrations of CDs, and 5 μg/mL was bright enough for cell imaging. The fluorescent emission spectra of CDs in cells were also explored. Fig. S12 demonstrated that the emission spectra of CDs remained almost unchanged whether in cells or solution, verifying the fine optical stability of CDs. Based on this, a series of diffraction-limited colocalization studies were conducted to validate the mitochondrial targeted ability of CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 , the bright yellow fluorescence of CDs in HeLa cells displayed clear filamentous shapes and overlapped well with the fluorescence of MTDR (a commercial mitochondria indicator), with a Pearson's colocalization coefficient (Rr) of 0.93, suggesting the majority of CDs were trapped into mitochondria. Similar results were acquired in other cell lines, including HepG-2 (Rr of 0.92), SMMC-7721 (Rr of 0.91), and HL-7702 (Rr of 0.92), hinting that the CDs distributed in not only mitochondria of cancer cells but also mitochondria of normal cells (Fig. S13). On the contrary, when HeLa cells were co-stained with CDs and LTDR (a commercial lysosome indicator) or Hoechst 33342 (Hoechst, a commercial nucleus dye), the Rr was 0.3 (for LTDR) and 0.03 (for Hoechst) individually, further indicating the CDs predominantly accumulated in mitochondria (Fig. 5B–C). Besides, the intracellular photostability of CDs was investigated. As seen from Fig. S14, CDs exhibited bright fluorescence after continuous irradiation for 30 scans, which could meet the demand for cell imaging. This result also suggested the good anti-photobleaching of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spatial locations of CDs with different MMP
ce:section.ce:para: MMP, which is dynamically transformed accompanied by cell viability, is fundamental for maintaining the normal function of mitochondria [44]. Substantial pieces of evidence has confirmed that mitochondrial depolarization could induce apoptosis, and MMP vanished in dead cells [45]. To accurately verify the responses of CDs to MMP, the distribution of CDs in different cell situations was investigated. As displayed in Fig. 6 , in healthy cells with normal MMP, the fluorescence of CDs exhibited evident filament structures, illustrating the location in mitochondria due to the electrostatic affinity between electropositive CDs and negative MMP. In addition, to obtain the damaged cells with reduced MMP, carbonyl cyanide 3-chlorophenylhydrazone (CCCP), as an uncoupler of MMP, was utilized to incubate the cells for 5 min to decrease MMP rapidly [46]. Obviously, partial CDs leaked from mitochondria and migrated into the nucleoli due to the reduced electrostatic attraction between CDs and MMP. Fig. S15 showed that CCCP has no effect on the fluorescence of CDs, thus the declining fluorescence of CDs in mitochondria could be attributed to the down-regulation of CDs after migration. In addition, the transfer of CDs into the nucleoli could be attributed to the electrostatic interaction between CDs and negative nucleic acids. Moreover, to acquire dead cells representing disappeared MMP, HeLa cells were treated with 4% paraformaldehyde. Intriguingly, weak fluorescence of CDs appeared in the cytoplasm, and most fluorescent signals concentrated in the nucleoli. Taken together, the varied MMP of cells could be identified through the different spatial distributions of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of viscosity and MMP
ce:section.ce:para: Monensin, a widely used anti-fungal drug, could disrupt the ionic balance, further trigger mitochondrial swelling and structural changes accompanied by viscosity enhancement. On the other hand, monensin could suppress gene expression, then induce apoptosis, which was related to the change of MMP [12,47]. Beyond that, LPS was usually employed to induce the increase of mitochondrial viscosity by producing inflammation. Moreover, the inflammation usually presented in the procedure of cell necrosis, which was accompanied by variational MMP [48]. Therefore, we predicted that the two drugs would cause simultaneous changes in mitochondrial viscosity and MMP. To verify the CD's ability of simultaneously detecting of mitochondrial viscosity and MMP and explore their associations, the simultaneous alternations of them was then evaluated. Before CDs were subjected to imaging of mitochondrial viscosity and MMP variations; we first verified that monensin and LPS had no interference on the fluorescence intensity of CDs in vitro (Figs. S16–17). Next, cells were sequentially cultured with drugs (monensin or LPS) and CDs. As depicted in Fig. 7 and Fig. S18, dim fluorescence in the mitochondria and no fluorescence in nucleoli were observed when the live cells were incubated with CDs only. In contrast, a remarkably enhanced fluorescence in the mitochondria and an appearance of fluorescent signal in nucleoli were obtained after the cells were treated with monensin or LPS. The fluorescence in nucleoli implied the lekage of CDs from mitochondria due to the decreased MMP. The enhanced fluorescence of CDs trapped in the mitochondria indicated the increase of viscosity. The results confirmed that the CDs were capable of effectively and simultaneously monitoring changes in mitochondrial viscosity and MMPs.

Inspired by the above results of cellular imaging, we next assessed the performance of CDs for imaging in living systems. As a vertebrate model, Zebrafish was adopted for in vivo studies owing to its high optical transparency and short growth period [49]. In brief, monensin, and LPS were used to increase the mitochondrial viscosity of zebrafish. Fig. S19 demonstrated that the remarkably enhanced fluorescence of zebrafish was observed compared with the zebrafish only treated with CDs. The results corresponded well with the phenomena of drug-stimulate cellular imaging, implying the CDs possessed the remarkable capability to monitor mitochondrial viscosity in vivo.

In summary, a new kind of fluorescent CDs were constructed to simultaneously monitor mitochondrial viscosity and MMP. The activable fluorescence of CDs was observed in high viscosity solvents compared with low viscosity solutions due to the prohibited rotation of pyrrolidine groups on the surface of CDs. The enhanced fluorescence was obtained with a good linear relationship with viscosity changing in the scope of 0.89–945 cP. Besides, the spatial distribution of CDs varied with the changed MMP. The CDs accumulated in mitochondria in live cells with natural MMP but accumulated in the nucleoli when cells died with disappeared MMP, and both mitochondria and nucleoli were occupied with CDs with decreased MMP. Thus, fluctuation of mitochondrial viscosity and MMP was successfully detected through the changed fluorescence signals as well as spatial location of CDs. Moreover, an increased mitochondrial viscosity accompanying a decreased MMP were simultaneously visualized within drug-mediated cells. Besides, an increasing viscosity of zebrafish was discriminated using CDs, verifying the application of CDs in vivo. Based on integration advantages of high sensitivity, outstanding biocompatibility, and eminent optical ability, we envision that the CDs can be a robust tool for exploring the biological events involved with mitochondrial viscosity and MMP, which may provide valuable information for facilitating the study of mitochondrial-related pathology and pharmacy.

Shuo Guo: Investigation, Material preparation, Writing – original draft, Software. Yuanqiang Sun: Supervision, Review, Editing. Zhaofan Wu: Investigation, Material preparation. Ran Yang: Supervision, Validation, Formal analysis. Lingbo Qu: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Resources. Zhaohui Li: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [21974125], Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Henan Province (22TRTSTHN002), the 111 Project of Henan Province [CXJD2021001], and the Collaborative Innovation Project of Zhengzhou (Zhengzhou University) [18XTZX12002].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2022.04.006.
